subject: english
title: PSLE English — Synthesis & Transformation Tricks (10 marks)

stats.headline: Synthesis = 10 marks. PSLE rotates the same 4 grammar patterns every year. Learn the keyword-to-pattern map and the most common traps — full marks become straightforward.

keyConcepts.title: Welcome — Synthesis & Transformation (10 marks)
keyConcepts.body: Synthesis asks you to REWRITE a sentence using a given keyword, keeping the meaning. 5 patterns cover ~90% of past PSLE questions. The hardest part isn't grammar — it's not changing meaning or adding info.
bullets: **Pattern A: Concession** — Although / Despite / In spite of
**Pattern B: Cause/Effect** — Because / Due to / Owing to / Since
**Pattern C: Condition** — If / Unless / Provided that
**Pattern D: Word transformation** — noun ↔ verb / active ↔ passive
**Pattern E: Reported Speech** — direct ↔ indirect (with backshift)

**The 3 rules of synthesis:**
① Don't change the meaning. ② Don't change the tense. ③ Don't add info.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern A — Concession (Although / Despite / In spite of)
keyConcepts.body: "Concession" = two ideas that contrast (one positive, one negative). The most common PSLE trap: students mix up the grammar after Although vs Despite/In spite of.
bullets:
**Although + CLAUSE** (subject + verb)
✓ Although **he was tired**, he kept working.
✗ Although **his tiredness**, he kept working.

**Despite / In spite of + NOUN PHRASE** (no subject + verb!)
✓ Despite **his tiredness**, he kept working.
✓ Despite **being tired**, he kept working.   (-ing form OK)
✗ Despite **he was tired**, he kept working.

**Conversion trick** (Although → Despite):
-- Change the verb to a noun OR an -ing form
-- Drop the subject (or change to "his/her/their + noun")
-- E.g. "Although it was raining" → "Despite the rain" / "Despite the fact that it was raining"

keyConcepts.title: Pattern B — Cause (Because / Due to / Owing to / Since)
keyConcepts.body: Same grammar split as concession. Because takes a clause; Due to and Owing to take a noun phrase.
bullets:
**Because / Since / As + CLAUSE** (subject + verb)
✓ Because **the bus broke down**, we were late.

**Due to / Owing to + NOUN PHRASE**
✓ Due to **the bus breakdown**, we were late.
✓ Owing to **his illness**, he missed school.
✗ Due to **the bus broke down**, we were late.

**Conversion trick** (Because → Due to): same as Pattern A
Verb → noun/-ing, drop the subject.
Example: "Because she was sick" → "Due to her illness" / "Due to being sick"
**Pattern A + B share the same grammar trick.** That's why PSLE drills both.

interactiveQuiz.label: Concession — Although → Despite
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: Although the weather was bad, the football match continued.
Rewrite using "Despite". Which is correct?
options: Despite the weather was bad, the football match continued.
Despite the bad weather, the football match continued.
Despite the football match, the weather was bad.
Despite of the bad weather, the football match continued.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: Despite takes a NOUN PHRASE. "The weather was bad" (clause) becomes "the bad weather" (noun phrase). Note: "Despite of" is always wrong — just "Despite," not "Despite of."

interactiveQuiz.label: Cause — Because → Due to
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: Because the road was flooded, the bus could not pass.
Rewrite using "Due to". Which is correct?
options: Due to the road was flooded, the bus could not pass.
Due to the flooded road, the bus could not pass.
Due to that the road was flooded, the bus could not pass.
Due to flooding the road, the bus could not pass.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: Due to + noun phrase. "The road was flooded" → "the flooded road" (adjective + noun). Option 4 changes the meaning ("flooding the road" implies someone is doing the flooding).

keyConcepts.title: Pattern C — Condition (If / Unless / Provided that)
keyConcepts.body: Conditional sentences. The Unless trap catches most students: "Unless" already means "if not", so you must NEGATE the if-clause when converting.
bullets:
**If + present, will + verb** (standard conditional)
✓ If you hurry, you will catch the bus.

**Unless = If...not** — flip the negation!
✓ Unless you hurry, you will miss the bus.   (= If you don't hurry...)
✗ Unless you don't hurry, you will catch the bus.   (double negative — wrong)

**Provided that = If (more formal)**
✓ You will catch the bus provided that you hurry.   (same meaning as "if")

**Conversion trick** (If → Unless):
-- Negate the if-clause (drop "not" if there is one; add "not" if there isn't)
-- Keep the main clause the same
Example: "If you don't study, you will fail" → "Unless you study, you will fail"

keyConcepts.title: Pattern D — Word transformation (noun ↔ verb, active ↔ passive)
keyConcepts.body: PSLE often gives a sentence and asks you to rewrite using a different WORD CLASS — usually noun ↔ verb. The trick: keep the meaning identical, change only the grammar.
bullets:
**Noun ↔ Verb pairs** (common in PSLE):
-- decide → decision     -- arrive → arrival
-- explain → explanation -- succeed → success
-- behave → behaviour    -- agree → agreement
-- discover → discovery  -- choose → choice

**Active ↔ Passive — keep the doer if asked, drop it if not**
Active: The boy broke the window.
Passive: The window **was broken** by the boy.   (was/were + past participle)
Active: They build a school every year.
Passive: A school **is built** every year.   (drop "by them" — generic)

**Comparative ↔ Superlative transformation:**
"No one runs faster than him." → "He runs the fastest."
"Nothing is more delicious than mum's cooking." → "Mum's cooking is the most delicious."
**Trap**: don't change the TENSE. If original is past, the rewrite must also be past.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern E — Reported Speech (Direct ↔ Indirect) + the past-past trap
keyConcepts.body: Convert direct speech (with quotes) to indirect (no quotes). The KEY rule is BACKSHIFT — tenses shift one step into the past when the reporting verb (said / told / asked) is in past tense.
bullets:
**Backshift table** (reporting verb is past):
-- present → past:        "I am happy" → he said he **was** happy
-- present continuous → past continuous: "I am eating" → he said he **was eating**
-- past → past perfect:   "I went home" → he said he **had gone** home
-- will → would:          "I will help" → he said he **would help**
-- can → could:           "I can swim" → he said he **could swim**

**⚠️ THE PAST-PAST TRAP** (most common mistake)
When the event happened **BEFORE** the speech, AND the speech was
in past tense → the event must be **past perfect** (had + past
participle). Not just simple past.
Direct: "I **broke** the window," John said.
✗ John said that he **broke** the window. (loses time-ordering)
✓ John said that he **had broken** the window. (past perfect = before "said")

**Pronoun + time/place shifts:**
-- I → he/she · you → I/me · we → they
-- today → that day · tomorrow → the next day · here → there · this → that

**Questions in reported speech** — use `asked if / whether`:
-- Direct: "Are you coming?" she asked.
-- Reported: She asked **if/whether** I **was** coming.
-- Direct: "Where do you live?" he asked.
-- Reported: He asked **where** I **lived**.   (NO question mark)

**Commands** — use `told [person] to + verb`:
-- Direct: "Sit down!" the teacher said.
-- Reported: The teacher **told** us **to sit down**.

interactiveQuiz.label: Unless negation trap
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: If you don't wear a raincoat, you will get wet.
Rewrite starting with "Unless". Which is correct?
options: Unless you don't wear a raincoat, you will get wet.
Unless you wear a raincoat, you will not get wet.
Unless you wear a raincoat, you will get wet.
Unless you wear a raincoat, you will be wet.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: "Unless" already means "if not", so DROP the "don't" when converting. "If you don't wear" → "Unless you wear". The main clause stays the same. Don't add a second negative or you flip the meaning.

interactiveQuiz.label: Noun ↔ verb transformation
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: The principal decided that the trip would be cancelled.
Rewrite using the word "decision". Which is correct?
options: The principal's decision was to cancel the trip.
The principal made a decision the trip would be cancelled.
The decision of the principal was the cancellation.
The principal had a decision to cancel the trip.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: "Decided that" → "decision was to + verb". Option 2 is missing "that" and reads ungrammatically. Option 3 changes the meaning (the decision IS the cancellation, vs. the principal decided to cancel). Option 4 is colloquial English, not PSLE-acceptable.

keyConcepts.title: The 3 most common synthesis mistakes (don't lose easy marks)
keyConcepts.body: Even students who know the grammar lose marks on these 3 things. Read each rewrite TWICE before submitting and check for them.
bullets:
**Mistake 1: Changing the tense**
Original: He **completed** his homework.
✗ He **completes** his homework.
✓ His homework **was completed**.
Rule: the rewrite's tense MUST match the original.

**Mistake 2: Adding information not in the original**
Original: She was late because of the traffic.
✗ She was late because of the heavy traffic this morning.
Rule: don't add adjectives, time, or details the original didn't have.

**Mistake 3: Changing the meaning subtly**
Original: Unless you hurry, you will miss the train.
✗ If you hurry, you will not miss the train.   (positive vs negative emphasis differs!)
✓ If you don't hurry, you will miss the train.
Rule: when in doubt, mentally check "does the rewrite say EXACTLY the same thing?"

**Check before submitting**:
① Same tense? ② Same info (no additions)? ③ Same meaning?

interactiveQuiz.label: Tense + info trap
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: The teacher praised the student because she scored full marks.
Rewrite starting with "Due to". Which is correct AND keeps tense + info?
options: Due to her perfect score, the teacher praised the student.
Due to scoring full marks, the student was praised by the teacher.
Due to her excellent performance, the teacher praised the bright student.
Due to the student scoring well, the teacher praised her.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: (1) changes "full marks" to "perfect score" — meaning shift. (3) adds "excellent" and "bright" — extra info. (4) waters down "full marks" to "well" — meaning shift. (2) preserves tense (past), keeps info ("full marks" / "scoring full marks"), and matches grammar.

keyConcepts.title: Start practising!
keyConcepts.body: Mixed practice drawn from PSLE 2016-2025 Synthesis & Transformation. All 4 patterns + the 3 common mistakes. Trains the reflex of checking "same tense, same info, same meaning."
bullets: **15 questions** — Concession (4) + Cause (3) + Condition (3) + Transform (5)
Wrong answers auto-saved, reviewed in 1 week
AI marks word-for-word — partial credit if you almost got it but missed tense/info

keyWords.definition: Two contrasting ideas joined: 'tired BUT kept going'. Keywords: Although + clause / Despite + noun phrase / In spite of + noun phrase. The grammar split (clause vs noun phrase) is the most common trap.
Same grammar split as concession. Because/Since/As take a clause (subject + verb). Due to / Owing to take a noun phrase. Convert verb → noun + drop subject.
Means 'if...not'. When converting If → Unless, NEGATE the if-clause (drop 'not' or add 'not'). Double negatives are the #1 trap.
Active: subject DOES the action ('the boy broke the window'). Passive: subject RECEIVES the action ('the window was broken by the boy'). Use was/were + past participle. Drop 'by X' if X is generic.
① Don't change the tense. ② Don't add info. ③ Don't change the meaning. Every rewrite must pass all three checks before you submit.

subTopics.label: Concession (Although/Despite)
subTopics.description: Two contrasting ideas. Although + clause vs Despite/In spite of + noun phrase. Conversion trick: verb → noun/-ing form, drop subject. PSLE's most-tested pattern.

subTopics.label: Cause (Because/Due to)
subTopics.description: Same grammar split as concession. Because/Since/As + clause; Due to/Owing to + noun phrase. Same conversion technique applies.

subTopics.label: Condition (If/Unless)
subTopics.description: Unless = if + not. When converting If → Unless, you must NEGATE the if-clause. Double-negative trap is common.

subTopics.label: Word transformation
subTopics.description: Noun ↔ verb (decide → decision), active ↔ passive (broke → was broken), comparative ↔ superlative. Most common pair set drilled in PSLE.

subTopics.label: 3 common mistakes
subTopics.description: Even students who know the grammar lose marks here: ① changing tense ② adding info ③ subtle meaning shift. Always check all three before submitting.
